content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 1, 2018

Karin Salameh
Assistant City Attorney
City of Monterey
City Hall
Monterey, CA 93940

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-18-204

Dear Ms. Salameh:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding Government Code Section 1090, et seq.¹ We are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

In accordance with Section 1097.1, subdivision (c)(3), we have forwarded your Section 1090 request, including all pertinent facts, to the Attorney General's Office and the Monterey County District Attorney's Office. We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) Our advice regarding Section 1090 "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTION

Does Section 1090 prohibit the City of Monterey (the City) from entering a prospective contract (Contract 2) with the Wallace Group (Wallace) to prepare plans, specifications and estimations for the Presidio of Monterey (POM) storm drain system repair project (the POM Project) where Wallace previously provided a study, survey and report on some, but not all, of the storm drains in the POM Project (Contract 1)?

CONCLUSION

No. Section 1090 does not prohibit the City from entering into Contract 2 with Wallace because, based on the facts provided, Wallace did not participate in the making of Contract 2 through its performance of Contract 1, and Wallace has not imposed considerable or undue influence over the City regarding Contract 2 or in procuring Contract 2 by the work it performed under Contract 1.

___________________________
¹ All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-18-204
Page No. 2

FACTS PROVIDED BY THE REQUESTOR

Pursuant to an Intergovernmental Support Agreement (IGSA) with the United States
government acting through the Secretary of the Army, the City provides installation support
services at the Presidio of Monterey (the POM). Several years ago, the POM began a multi-year
planning effort to identify infrastructure improvements necessary for planned short-range and long-
range development, including its storm drain system.

A.     The 2014 Agreement to Study, Survey and Report.

As part of this effort, the City issued a request for proposals (RFP) in 2013 to perform a
storm water drain line condition survey for the POM. The RFP sought "proposals from qualified
consultants to survey, inspect, collect data and report on the existing storm drain infrastructure
within at least three (3) water sheds" at the POM. The RFP further explained that "the study and
survey will be used for current and future development, and budgeting purposes to rehabilitate
and/or upgrade the existing storm drain systems."

The City received three proposals in response to the RFP and selected Wallace as the most
qualified. On June 24, 2014, the City entered into a Professional Services Agreement with Wallace
to perform the POM storm drain survey for Zones 1, 2, and 3. Specifically, Wallace was tasked
with: (1) assessing the condition of the storm drain pipes and structures; (2) updating the
Geographic Information System (GIS) database with the data collected in the field; and
(3) submitting a condition assessment report identifying and priority ranking future capital
improvement projects.

Wallace prepared a condition assessment report that assigned each storm drain feature
surveyed a category from 1-5 as follows:

• Category 1 structures were clean and undamaged; no additional resources were
recommended for these structures.
• Category 2 structures were found to have debris, but were undamaged. It was recommended
that the City budget to clean these facilities in the future.
• Category 3 structures did not have any debris, but were damaged and recommended for
replacement.
• Category 4 structures had debris and were damaged. These structures were recommended
for replacement, but if that was not occurring in the near future, then the recommendation
was for interim cleaning.
• Category 5 structures were facilities that should be cleaned, but damage could not be
assessed and the condition was unknown. The report recommended that these facilities be
cleaned and then re-assessed to determine their condition.

Within the categories, the Wallace report gave the structures priority rankings according to
which should be cleaned or replaced first and provided cost estimates for these tasks. For example,
structures in Category 3 or 4 where damage was found would be assigned as Priority 4 "severe,"
Priority 3 "moderate," etc. In July 2016, Wallace submitted its final report (the Report) and the
contract was closed.

File No. A-18-204
Page No. 3

B.      The Prospective RFP and Agreement for Plans, Specifications and Estimations.

       On July 30, 2018, the Army issued an Individual Job Order under the IGSA to the City to
repair damaged storm drain lines and storm drain structures. The Army has defined the scope of the
project based upon the 2016 Wallace Report's categorization and priority recommendations to
include damaged pipes listed as replacement priority 3 and 4 and damaged storm drain structures
listed as priority 2, 3, and 4. Additionally, the Army included three failed storm drains that were
discovered by City staff after the condition survey and thus not part of the Wallace Report.

       City staff is in the process of preparing an RFP for a consultant to prepare full plans,
specifications, and estimations for the POM Project. The Wallace Report will be attached to the
RFP. Once that work is completed, a separate call for bids will be issued to perform the physical
repair work based upon the prepared plans and specifications. The City anticipates that Wallace
may submit a proposal to prepare plans, specifications, and estimations for the POM Project.

ANALYSIS

A.      Wallace is Not Subject to Section 1090.

       1. Legal standard for independent contractor under Section 1090.

       Interpreting the term "employees" as used in Section 1090, the California Supreme Court
recently affirmed the long-standing rule from case law that independent contractors are not
categorically excluded from Section 1090: "Liability under the statute can extend to independent
contractors who have duties to engage in or advise on public contracting." (People v. Superior
Court (Sahlolbei) (2017) 3 Cal.5th 230, 233.) For example, an independent contractor for a state or
local government agency that "has a hand in designing and developing the plans and specifications
for the project," has made or participated in the making of a contract for the construction of the
project and is therefore prohibited from entering a contract to complete the project. (Davis v. Fresno
Unified School District (2015) 237 Cal.App.4th 261, 300-301.)

       However, not all independent contractors are covered by section 1090. (Sahlolbei, supra, at
p. 240.) "'An individual's status as an official under [section 1090] turns on the extent to which the
person influences an agency's contracting decisions or otherwise acts in a capacity that demands the
public trust.'" (Ibid, quoting Hub City Solid Waste Services, Inc. v. City of Compton (Hub City)
(2010) 186 Cal.App.4th 1114, 1125.) In Hub City, the court held that an independent contractor that
exerts "considerable influence over the contracting decisions of a public agency" is subject to
Section 1090. (Hub City, supra, at pp. 1124-1125.)

       Previously, we have advised that consultants who play a limited technical role and are
removed from influencing the contracting decisions of a public agency may not be subject to
Section 1090. (La Salle Advice Letter, No. A-17-074 and Green Advice Letter, No. A-16-084.)
Likewise, in the Chadwick Advice Letter, No. A-15-147, we determined that only the primary
consultant and a "highly involved sub-consultant" were subject to Section 1090. The sub-
consultants who provided technical input, reports, and similar information in a support role to the
consultants, were not subject to Section 1090.

File No. A-18-204
Page No. 4

Generally, an independent contractor is prohibited from a subsequent project to complete a project only if the contractor has had extensive involvement in the initial design and develop of the project and the nature of the contractor's involvement gives the contractor undue influence over the public entity in procuring the contract.

2. Wallace did not exert influence over the City's contracting decisions.

Based on the facts provided, which includes copies of Contract 1, the Report, and the Army's Individual Job Order, Wallace did not exert "considerable influence over the contracting decisions of a public agency" related to Contract 2 because of the work it perform in Contract 1. Moreover, Wallace did not "engage in or advise on public contracting" based on the scope of work it performed in Contract 1. The subject of Contract 1 was an assessment and inventory report. It was not a Master Plan that set design criteria, nor did it involve recommendations that would show that Wallace unduly influenced the City by providing engineering services for design of structures assessed, inventoried and prioritized. In addition, Contract 2 would call for plans, specifications, and estimations of three storm drains identified after Wallace issued its Report.

Contract 1 did not include preparation of plans, specifications or estimations for the POM Project, but was limited to a preliminary evaluation of the existing storm drain system. The RFP scope of work required site visits to determine priority ranking for improvement of the storm drain system. Contract 1 required Wallace to provide improvement cost estimates for preliminary design, engineering, administration, inspection and construction relevant to construction and repair work to be performed after the preliminary work under Contract 2 is complete.

The facts you provided do not show that the Report and the work that is the subject of Contract 1 permitted Wallace to influence the City in the selection processes for Contract 2. The two contracts would involve separate and distinct scopes of work. Contract 1 required assessment for priority ranking, surveys, inspection and a written report. Contract 2 will require preparation of plans, specifications, and estimations for the POM Project that are preliminary to the repair work to be performed under a separate contract.

The City will include a copy of the Report with the RFP. Providing background information in an RFP is standard industry practice. The Army's Individual Job Order was based primarily on the Wallace Report, including a copy of the Report with the RFP provides potential respondents with the background information needed to consider the conduct and submit a bid. Sharing this information, to the extent it is relevant to the scope of work, creates a level playing field among the respondents and eliminates any undue influence that Wallace could have over the City by preparing the Report.

B. Wallace Has Not Participated in the Making of Contract 2.

Participation in the making of a contract is defined broadly as any act involving preliminary discussions, negotiations, compromises, reasoning, planning, drawing of plans and specifications, and solicitation of bids. (Milbrae Assn. for Residential Survival v. City of Millbrae (1968) 262 Cal.App.2d 222, 237.)

File No. A-18-204
Page No. 5

Wallace provided a Report that allowed the Army to prepare the Individual Job Order. The work Wallace performed under Contract 1, and the work that will be required under Contract 2, are distinctly different in scope, detail and purpose. For instance, Wallace did not prepare any plans or specifications for the storm drain system under Contract 1. The Report contained only a possible layout of improvements sufficiently detailed to determine a preliminary budgetary cost estimate. The Report shows no elevations, engineering drawing sections, or geotechnical information. The Report is not a plan or a specification for the POM Project. The scope of work contained in the RFP will require the successful respondent to make a de novo evaluation of the storm drain system.

The facts provided indicate that Wallace did not unduly influence the City to procure Contract 2 based on the work performed in Contract 1. For example: (1) Wallace has not had preliminary discussions with the City regarding Contract 2; (2) Wallace has engaged in no negotiations with the City regarding Contract 2; (3) Wallace has not consulted with the City regarding Contract 2; and (4) Wallace has engaged in no planning with the City regarding Contract 2. Thus, Section 1090 does not prohibit the City from entering Contract 2 with Wallace.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: John M. Feser Jr.
Senior Commission Counsel
Legal Division

JMF:jgl
